narration: Comment out the else and finally blocks to focus on try and except first.
Attempt to open a file with an incorrect filename to demonstrate a FileNotFoundError.
Observe the traceback error generated by Python when the file is not found.
Move the file opening code into a try block to handle potential errors.
Add an except block that prints a custom error message like 'Sorry this file does not exist'.
Run the code to verify that the custom error message is displayed instead of the traceback.
Introduce a NameError inside the try block to demonstrate that a general exception catches all errors.
Change the except block to specifically catch FileNotFoundError instead of a general exception.
Run the code again to see that the NameError is no longer caught and produces a traceback.
Add a second except block to catch general exceptions for errors not covered by specific handlers.
Ensure specific exceptions are listed before general exceptions to prevent the general handler from catching everything.
Use the 'as' keyword in the except block to assign the exception object to a variable like 'e'.
Print the exception variable 'e' to display the specific error details instead of a custom message.
Uncomment the else block to demonstrate code that runs only if no exception occurs in the try block.
Place file reading and closing logic inside the else block to ensure it only executes on success.
Uncomment the finally block to demonstrate code that executes regardless of whether an exception occurred.
Use the finally block for cleanup tasks like closing database connections or files.
Create a conditional check inside the try block to detect a specific file name like 'corrupt_file.txt'.
Use the 'raise' keyword to manually trigger an exception when the specific condition is met.
Run the code to verify that the manually raised exception is caught by the appropriate except block.
